chunk_summary: This section outlines dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation guidelines for Indian practice, emphasizing standardization, nutrient mapping, feeding safety, and public health alignment using authoritative references like IAP, WHO, and ICMR-NIN.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of standardization in dietary assessment and feeding counseling for infants and young children.
Learn to apply WHO/UNICEF indicators (MDD, MMF, MAD) for documenting feeding outcomes.
Familiarize with Indian food composition resources (IFCT, NIN) for nutrient estimation and dietary mapping.
Recognize multidisciplinary approaches for feeding safety, including IDDSI texture standards and pediatric swallowing assessments.
key_facts: Timely introduction of complementary feeding is recommended at about 6 months of age (IAP, WHO).
WHO/UNICEF indicators include Minimum Dietary Diversity (MDD), Minimum Meal Frequency (MMF), and Minimum Acceptable Diet (MAD).
Indian food composition resources like IFCT and NIN are essential for nutrient mapping and dietary guidance.
National platforms such as ICDS/POSHAN and anemia control programs from MoHFW provide parallel counseling for families.
IDDSI framework standardizes food and liquid texture terminology for feeding safety across teams.
reference_quote: For infants and young children, align dietary assessment and counseling with IAP infant and young child feeding guidance and the WHO complementary feeding guideline, which emphasize timely introduction at about 6 months, diet diversity, meal frequency, and responsive feeding.
document_key_topics: Dietary assessment and counseling for infants and young children
Nutrient mapping using Indian food composition resources
Public health platforms and feeding program linkage
